title: Pastor Who Knew Kept Quiet, Then Kept His Job, Then Didn't: Brady Boyd's New Life Ends Early
teaser: Brady Boyd, senior pastor of New Life Church in Colorado Springs, resigned in June 2025 after court documents revealed he had known about fellow pastor Robert Morris's prior misconduct for years. Church leadership concluded Boyd had shared inaccurate information with the congregation and could no longer be trusted in the role.
body:
There is a certain kind of accountability that arrives not through confession but through subpoena. Brady Boyd, who served as senior pastor of New Life Church in Colorado Springs, Colorado, discovered this the hard way in June 2025. Court documents connected to the legal case involving Robert Morris of Gateway Church surfaced information that Boyd had reportedly been aware of Morris's misconduct for years, including during the period when Boyd himself was being recruited by New Life Church in 2007.

According to reporting by Juicy Ecumenism, a publication of the Institute on Religion and Democracy, church officials determined that Boyd had provided inaccurate information to his congregation regarding what he knew and when he knew it. The finding was significant enough that leadership concluded Boyd was not fully trustworthy before the people he had been shepherding. That is a fairly damning institutional verdict for a pastor whose job description centers on moral authority and relational trust.

The timeline here deserves close attention. The recruitment process for Boyd's position at New Life Church reportedly took place in 2007, which means the knowledge of Morris's misconduct, if accurately described in the court record, predates nearly two decades of Boyd's tenure at the church. New Life Church itself carries its own fraught institutional history, having been rocked years earlier by the resignation of founding pastor Ted Haggard in 2006 amid its own misconduct revelations. Boyd was brought in, at least in part, to restore credibility to a congregation that had already been through a defining betrayal.

The irony of that institutional context is not subtle. A congregation that had already absorbed one high-profile pastoral scandal was led for years by a man who, according to court documents, had withheld relevant information about a peer's misconduct. Whether Boyd's silence constituted active concealment or a more passive form of omission is a distinction the congregation is presumably now working through. What is not in dispute, based on available reporting, is that church leadership found his account of events to be inaccurate.

Robert Morris resigned from Gateway Church in Southlake, Texas amid his own misconduct allegations, and the legal aftermath of that case has continued to generate disclosures affecting figures connected to Morris. Boyd appears to be among those whose prior knowledge came into sharper focus as litigation proceeded. It is a reminder that legal discovery has a way of reconstructing institutional memory that informal networks of ministerial silence tend to obscure.

Boyd stepped down on June 8, 2025. The church's determination that he had shared inaccurate information with the congregation is, in the language of ecclesiastical accountability, roughly equivalent to a finding of dishonesty with the people he was entrusted to lead. Pastors routinely preach on the virtue of truth-telling, the sanctity of transparency, and the spiritual dangers of concealment. Boyd's ministry at New Life was long and, by most external measures, stable. The circumstances of its end complicate that legacy considerably.

It is worth noting what the available reporting does not establish. There is no published account indicating that Boyd was himself an alleged perpetrator of misconduct. The case against him, as documented, is one of knowledge withheld and information inaccurately conveyed. That is a narrower charge than direct wrongdoing, but in a pastoral context, the withholding of relevant information about a colleague's behavior toward others is not a minor administrative lapse. It is a breach of the protective function a church leader is expected to serve.

The Institute on Religion and Democracy, through its Juicy Ecumenism reporting arm, has covered the downstream consequences of the Morris case extensively. Boyd's situation illustrates a recurring dynamic in large evangelical institutions: misconduct rarely stays contained to a single actor, and the people positioned to have intervened earlier often remain in place until external legal pressure forces disclosure. The court system, in this instance, accomplished what internal church accountability did not.

New Life Church now faces the task of explaining to its congregation not only that its pastor resigned but that the stated reason involves a finding of inaccuracy and diminished trustworthiness. That framing, which came from church leadership rather than from Boyd's critics, places the institution in the position of having to account for its own oversight failure. A church does not typically announce that its senior pastor was not fully trustworthy before the congregation unless it has concluded there is no more comfortable way to frame what happened.

Boyd's tenure at New Life lasted roughly eighteen years, a span that encompasses an enormous volume of sermons, counseling sessions, public statements, and institutional decisions. The congregation is left to assess which parts of that record to weigh against the disclosure now on the table. That is not a comfortable exercise, and it is not one that resolves quickly. Congregations that have been through this kind of leadership rupture tend to carry the fracture lines for years.

The self-own here operates on several levels simultaneously. Boyd was a pastor at a church defined by a prior scandal who spent years in a position of trust while holding information that, had it been disclosed, would have been directly relevant to his own fitness for the role. The court documents did not create the problem; they revealed it. That distinction matters, because it suggests the accountability deficit was not a product of external attack but of internal concealment meeting external legal process at an inconvenient intersection.

Whatever Boyd's private accounting of his decisions looks like, the public record now includes a church leadership determination that he was not fully trustworthy before the congregation. For a pastor, that sentence is a kind of institutional obituary for the role itself. New Life Church has been through this particular kind of grief before, which may give it some capacity to absorb the rupture. Whether that constitutes resilience or simply familiarity with institutional betrayal is a theological question the congregation will have to answer for itself.
